w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh wp14

w:r.w:t: The user submits a textual description of the store idea through the system interface.
This description may include general information such as the type of products, the target audience, or the overall concept of the store.
After submitting the request, the system authenticates the user and extracts the
from the authentication context to ensure proper data isolation between tenants.
The request is then forwarded to the
, which is responsible for generating the initial store draft.
The system sends a generation request to the AI service containing the store description provided by the user.
Within the same request, the AI analyzes the description to determine whether the provided information is sufficient to generate an initial store draft.
The AI response may result in one of the following outcomes:
, the AI directly generates the store draft.
, the AI returns clarification questions to collect the missing information.
If the AI determines that the description is not sufficient, the system presents a set of
These questions are designed to collect the essential information required to generate the store configuration, such as:
The user selects the appropriate answers, and the responses are merged with the original store description.
The updated input is then sent again to the same AI generation process.
This clarification cycle continues until sufficient information is available for the AI to generate the store draft.
4. Store Draft Generation and Interactive Presentation
At this stage, after the initial AI-generated store draft is presented, the user has four interaction options:
The user can directly modify any field (e.g., store name, description, product price).
The user confirms the draft as-is, which triggers store creation.
Partial regeneration allows the user to request AI assistance to regenerate a
within the store draft while preserving the rest of the configuration.
Unlike manual editing, partial regeneration represents a
that triggers a new request to the AI service.
When the user selects the regeneration option for a specific field, the system constructs a new AI request containing the following context:
the current store metadata (e.g., store name, description, category)
the full current store draft structure
the specific field that should be regenerated
constraints specifying which fields must remain unchanged
The AI service is explicitly instructed to
while maintaining compatibility with the rest of the draft.
After receiving the response, the system performs validation using the
the regenerated field respects the defined data type and constraints
the generated value remains consistent with the store category and other related fields
If the validation succeeds, the regenerated field replaces the previous value in the store draft.
If validation fails, the system rejects the generated value and allows the user to retry regeneration or continue editing manually.
Each partial regeneration request is executed within the
, ensuring that the AI receives the most recent version of the store draft and maintains continuity throughout the editing process.
Full regeneration allows the user to request the AI to
generate a completely new store draft
This option is useful when the user is not satisfied with the initial AI output and prefers to receive a new alternative configuration.
When full regeneration is requested, the system constructs a new AI request containing:
the original store description provided by the user
the current store category or constraints defined during the session
The AI service then generates a
Once the new draft is generated, the system performs
using the Store Draft Validation Schema to verify that all required fields, data types, and structural constraints are satisfied.
If the generated draft passes validation, it replaces the previous draft and is presented to the user for further review.
If validation fails, the system rejects the generated output and redirects the process to
Partial Regeneration or the Fallback Flow
Like partial regeneration, full regeneration is executed within the
, ensuring that the AI generation process maintains the correct tenant context and session continuity.
Must belong to a predefined list of allowed categories
Must match one of the available system templates
Length 3–80 characters, must be unique within the store
Must match the store category or its subcategories
All products must belong to the
present and of the correct type
After receiving the AI-generated store draft, verify
If any validation rule fails, reject the draft and route to
Only after passing all validation rules is the draft presented to the user for review and editing.
After reviewing and optionally modifying the generated store draft, the user confirms the final store configuration.
Once the confirmation is received, the system creates the store according to the approved configuration and stores the data in the database.
All stored data is associated with the
To ensure system availability and prevent workflow interruption, the platform defines explicit fallback conditions and recovery mechanisms when the AI service fails or produces unusable responses.
The fallback mechanism is activated when any of the following conditions occur during interaction with the AI service:
If the AI service returns an HTTP error response such as 500, 502, 503, or 429, the request is considered failed.
: If the AI service does not respond within the defined time threshold (10 seconds), the request is considered timed out and is cancelled.
: If the AI response cannot be parsed, contains invalid JSON structure, or misses critical structural elements required by the system.
the generated store draft fails to pass the mandatory validation rules defined in the
, such as missing required fields or violating structural constraints.
When any of these conditions occur, the system redirects the request to the fallback flow.
When fallback is triggered, the platform provides alternative options that allow the user to continue the store creation process without relying on the AI service.
containing default categories and placeholder products
retry the AI generation process later
These mechanisms ensure that the store creation process remains functional even when the AI service becomes temporarily unavailable.

Before selecting the final architectural approach for the AI Store Creation Pipeline, multiple design alternatives were evaluated. Each alternative represents a different way of structuring the interaction between the system and the AI service to generate store drafts from user descriptions. The evaluation focused on balancing key concerns:

The following four options were considered, ranging from the simplest single-call approach to more complex multi-step and agent-based pipelines. Each option is analyzed in terms of its advantages and disadvantages to determine the most suitable architecture for the system.
A single request is sent to the LLM, which directly generates the complete store output
(metadata + mock products) based solely on the initial user input. No intermediate steps or interaction loops are involved.
No orchestration, no state management, no multi-step coordination.
t: If the user description lacks details, the output quality degrades significantly.
The system cannot refine or clarify requirements dynamically.
All logic is delegated to a single AI response.
The pipeline is broken into multiple sequential AI calls. Each step performs a specific task (e.g., extract intent
generate products), and the output of each step feeds into the next.
Each step can be validated and adjusted independently.
Clear separation of responsibilities within the pipeline.
Errors can be detected earlier in intermediate steps.
Multiple sequential AI calls increase total response time.
Requires orchestration logic and error handling between steps.
Failure in one step affects the entire pipeline.

The AI operates as an autonomous agent that iteratively plans, executes, and refines its output. It may perform multiple internal steps, retries, and decision-making loops while maintaining state across interactions.
Can adapt dynamically to complex or incomplete inputs.
Suitable for sophisticated multi-step generation tasks.
Requires state management, orchestration, and possibly external frameworks.
Adds unnecessary complexity for generating simple store drafts
Option D: Single Call + AI-Driven Clarification
A hybrid approach where the AI first evaluates the input. If insufficient, it generates clarification questions. After collecting user responses, a final AI call generates the store draft.
Only introduces additional steps when necessary.
AI guides the user instead of failing silently.
Avoids full multi-step or agentic pipelines.
Need logic for managing Q&A flow.
To determine the most appropriate AI pipeline design, the alternatives were evaluated based on several architectural criteria derived from the system requirements and quality attributes.
The evaluation considered the following aspects:
The pipeline should maintain a response time within the target range of 3–5 seconds for generating the initial store draft.
The system must be capable of handling incomplete or vague user input and guiding the user through clarification when necessary.
The solution should remain feasible within the scope and timeline of the project without introducing unnecessary architectural complexity.
The pipeline should support concurrent requests from multiple store owners without significant performance degradation.
The design should allow future modifications, such as extending the generation logic or introducing additional AI features.
The system should remain functional even when AI services fail or return invalid responses.
These criteria were used to evaluate the proposed pipeline alternatives and guide the final design decision
AI Pipeline Design Alternatives & Comparison Table
Option D: Single Call + AI-Driven Clarification
High – mostly one call, extra only if clarification needed
Low – very complex, heavy state management
Medium – sequential calls may slow under high load
High – mostly stateless, only conditional extra calls
Low – complex logic, harder to maintain
Single Call with AI-Driven Conditional Clarification and Interactive Refinement
Only introduces additional interaction when required
Users are not forced to provide all data upfront
AI dynamically guides the user through missing information
Interactive editing + partial regeneration enhances flexibility
Avoids over-engineering (no agentic loops or heavy orchestration)
Keeps implementation feasible within project scope
Supports concurrent tenant requests via horizontal scaling
Easy to extend (e.g., adding new fields or generation logic)
Validation ensures safe persistence of AI output
Fallback mechanism guarantees system availability even if AI fails

This design intentionally avoids complex AI orchestration (e.g., agentic loops) and instead adopts a controlled, interaction-driven approach that satisfies system requirements while maintaining simplicity, performance, and extensibility.

This approach was selected because the system primarily generates a relatively small amount of structured content (store metadata and a limited number of mock products). Therefore, a lightweight interaction-driven pipeline provides sufficient flexibility without introducing unnecessary architectural complexity.
